items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: How many resolved artworks does an IBDP HL exhibition require minimum?
items.why: Correct answer: 8. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1976 essay critiques the white cube gallery?
items.why: Correct answer: O'Doherty. “Krauss” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Documenta first open in Kassel?
items.why: Exact answer: 1955 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
